behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) from the description.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A unit test on signature parsing only verifies the parser, not whether the LLM follows the interface spec. LLM-DEP: The real failure mode is whether a real LLM reliably produces the correct signature from a natural language description. A stub that returns a well-formed signature always passes, hiding the actual failure. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the LLM response to a valid signature makes this test vacuous — it can never fail regardless of what a real LLM would produce. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Agentic, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Agentic. REJECTION-GATE: Integration is disqualified because no amount of in-process module wiring tests whether the real LLM respects parameter names and types from the spec.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real code generation service with a function description that specifies exact parameter names, types, and return type (e.g., 'def compute_discount(price: float, rate: float) -> float'). Extract the generated signature and assert it matches the specified interface exactly. Run N=10 times; assert >90% pass rate to account for non-determinism.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Correctness of the implementation depends on what the real LLM generates — a stub always returns passing code, hiding the real failure. REAL-INFRA: Running tests requires executing generated code in a subprocess or sandboxed environment; in-process fakes cannot reproduce real execution failures (import errors, runtime exceptions, wrong output). MULTI-STEP: This is explicitly the generate→test→refine→test loop — multiple real agentic steps in sequence. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Workflow, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Workflow. REJECTION-GATE: System is disqualified because this behavior's correctness depends on real LLM output quality across iterations, not just real subprocess behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a function description with a known test suite to the live service. Allow up to 3 iterations. Assert that at some attempt ≤3, the generated code passes all provided tests as verified by the real test runner. Use a problem that sometimes requires a refinement step to confirm the retry path also works.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is at the module boundary between the test runner output and the prompt builder — a unit test on the prompt builder alone only verifies formatting logic, not that real failure output actually flows through. LLM-DEP: Correctness here is about what is IN the prompt (the input to the LLM), not about the LLM's output quality. A captured/mock LLM call can verify the prompt contents. REAL-INFRA: The test runner can be faked to return controlled failure messages; no real subprocess is needed to verify prompt construction. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed — fake runner output suffices. (b) Verifying prompt contents does not depend on real AI output. (c) One refinement step, not a chain of real agentic steps. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Integration, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the orchestrator with a stub test runner that returns predetermined failure messages and a mock LLM client that captures the prompts it receives. Trigger one failed attempt, then inspect the prompt passed to the LLM on the second call and assert it contains the exact failure messages from the stub runner.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error containing all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode spans the orchestrator, LLM client, and test runner — specifically, whether the orchestrator correctly accumulates attempt records and terminates at the right boundary. A unit test on the orchestrator with injected fakes misses wiring bugs in how results are collected and surfaced. LLM-DEP: Correctness is about orchestration logic, not LLM output quality; a stub LLM that always returns failing code is sufficient. REAL-INFRA: No real subprocess or network needed; a stub test runner that always returns failures is sufficient to exercise the 3-attempt termination path. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network. (b) No real AI/ML output needed. (c) Three mocked iterations, not three real agentic steps. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Integration, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the orchestrator with a stub LLM that always returns syntactically valid but functionally wrong code, and a stub test runner that always returns the same failure. Invoke the service and assert: (a) exactly 3 attempts were made, (b) the returned error is structured with all 3 attempt payloads and their test results, (c) no 4th attempt occurs.

behaviors.description: The full generate→test→refine→test loop produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: This behavior is explicitly defined as a multi-step agentic pipeline: generate (real LLM call 1) → test (real code execution) → refine (real LLM call 2 with failure context) → test again (real code execution). The behavior's claim — that the loop succeeds where a single call fails — is only falsifiable with real agentic steps in sequence. LLM-DEP: Both LLM calls must be real; a stub that manufactures a 'fixed' response on the second call defeats the entire point of the test. REAL-INFRA: Executing generated code against a test suite requires real subprocess execution. EVIDENCE-LEVEL=Workflow, OUTPUT-LEVEL=Workflow. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic is insufficient because the behavior specifically asserts something about the sequential multi-step loop producing a result that a single call cannot — validating that claim requires at least two real LLM interactions in sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Select a known benchmark function where single-shot LLM generation reliably fails (e.g., a tricky edge case in string manipulation or numeric precision). Submit it to the live service with a complete test suite. Assert that the final result passes all tests AND that at least one refinement iteration occurred (i.e., the first attempt failed). Run N=5 times; assert >80% end-to-end pass rate.
